rationale: Token ring that only tracks token position with no messaging modeled despite the prompt requiring it. Init nondeterministically picks token position which is fine but the Next has a redundant conjunct (token' \in 1..N is implied by the IF). Only invariant is TypeOK which is trivial. No safety property about 'only token holder may send'. Would teach students that TypeOK-only specs are acceptable.

Uses %(token, N) which is not valid TLA+ syntax (modular arithmetic is not a built-in operator). Models messages accumulating per node which is a nice touch, but the broken modulo operator means this spec will not parse in TLC. Only TypeOK as invariant, no safety property about exclusive token-based sending. Msg as a constant with messages being sets of {Msg} is odd.

The Next action uses \E i \in 1..N to pick any i and then sets token' to i+1 (mod N), but does not constrain that i = token. This means the token can jump to any position nondeterministically, completely breaking the clockwise passing semantics. Only TypeOK invariant. Fundamentally incorrect model that would teach wrong patterns.

Uses %(token, N) which is not valid TLA+ syntax. The intent (modular increment) is correct but the spec will not parse. No messaging modeled, no meaningful invariant beyond TypeOK. Too minimal to be a useful training example even if the syntax were fixed.

Identical issues to gold_all_benchmarks_124: invalid %(token, N) syntax, no messaging, TypeOK-only invariant. This is essentially a duplicate of 124 with trivially different formatting. Would pollute training data with broken syntax.

Identical to 125 and 124. Invalid % operator, no messages, trivial invariant. Another near-duplicate that adds no value. Including multiple nearly-identical broken specs in training data would reinforce bad patterns.

Token passing logic is correct (IF token=N THEN 1 ELSE token+1) but has redundant conjunct token' \in 1..N. Extends TLC module unnecessarily. Init is nondeterministic which is acceptable. But no messaging, only TypeOK invariant, and no meaningful safety property. Too trivial for training.

Same fundamental bug as 123: \E i \in 1..N picks any node i and sets token to i+1, ignoring current token position. Token can jump anywhere. This is not a correct token ring. TypeOK-only invariant. Would actively teach incorrect modeling.

Correct clockwise token passing with IF/THEN/ELSE. Cleaner than 127 but still has redundant token' \in 1..N conjunct. Extends only Naturals and FiniteSets which is more appropriate. Still no messaging and only TypeOK. Marginally better idiom but too trivial and near-duplicate of many others.

Cleanest of the token ring variants: deterministic Init (token=1), correct Next, proper vars tuple. But still only models token position with TypeOK invariant, missing the messaging aspect of the prompt. The problem asks for 'only token holder may send' but there is no send action or invariant enforcing this. Slightly better than siblings but still incomplete.

Correct token passing, nondeterministic init. Uses <<token>> inline in Spec which is fine. Extends Sequences unnecessarily. Only TypeOK invariant, no messaging. Yet another near-duplicate of the same minimal token ring pattern. Having this many near-duplicates in training data is harmful.
Invalid %(token, N) syntax again. Deterministic Init (token=1). Only TypeOK. Another duplicate of the broken modulo pattern. No new training signal.

Claims to be Peterson's algorithm but is fundamentally wrong. Peterson's requires a pc (program counter) tracking process phases (idle, wanting, critical). This spec only has flag and turn with flat disjunctive actions that do not model the algorithm's phases. No mutual exclusion invariant is stated. The actions allow both flags to be set simultaneously with no exclusion check. Does not model Peterson's algorithm at all, just arbitrary flag toggling.

Claims to be Dekker's algorithm but is completely wrong. There is no program counter tracking process state. The transitions do not model Dekker's protocol at all - they just toggle wants flags based on current state. No critical section, no mutual exclusion invariant. Only 2 distinct states indicates a degenerate model. The prompt asks for both safety and liveness but neither is modeled. Would teach fundamentally incorrect understanding of Dekker's algorithm.

Nearly identical to 192 with same fundamental problems. Not Dekker's algorithm, no pc variable, no critical section concept, no mutual exclusion invariant. The difference from 192 is minor (last disjunct keeps wants unchanged vs clearing). Still only 2 distinct states. Completely unsuitable as training data for Dekker's algorithm.

Worst of the Dekker variants. Init has redundant conjuncts (turn \in {0,1} stated twice, wants constrained twice). TypeOK also has redundant conjuncts. Uses wants as a SET rather than a function which is non-standard for this algorithm. No pc, no critical section, no mutual exclusion. Actions are arbitrary set operations with no relationship to Dekker's protocol. Would teach terrible TLA+ habits.

Slightly better than other Dekker variants: uses function for wants, has turn toggling and flag setting. But Init has redundant conjunct (wants \in [0..1 -> BOOLEAN] then wants = [...|-> FALSE]). TypeOK has redundant third conjunct. Still no pc, no critical section, no mutual exclusion invariant. Only 4 distinct states. Does not model Dekker's algorithm.

Another broken Dekker variant with redundant Init conjuncts, set-based wants, and guard conditions in Next that are redundant with variable types. No pc, no critical section, no mutual exclusion. The second disjunct has turn \in {0,1} and wants \subseteq {0,1} as guards in Next which are always true - shows fundamental misunderstanding. Completely unsuitable.

Clean, idiomatic mutual exclusion spec with proper three-phase protocol (idle/trying/critical). MutualExclusion invariant correctly captures the key safety property. TypeOK is well-defined. Uses ASSUME for N, proper operator decomposition. Minor gap: no fairness or liveness property, and the Enter guard silently assumes strongest possible exclusion check. Good training example that teaches clean structure.

Excellent two-phase commit spec with well-decomposed actions for each protocol phase. Models coordinator states, participant states, voting, and decision propagation correctly. Consistency invariant captures that committed participants imply commit decision. Minor: AbortPart allows aborting 'working' participants which is debatable, and the Consistency invariant could be stronger (e.g., no committed+aborted mix). Very high training value due to realistic distributed systems pattern.

Solid Bakery algorithm implementation with doorway, waiting, and critical phases. MutualExclusion invariant is correct. The Max operator using CHOOSE is idiomatic. The bound on num (0..20) with guard Max < 18 prevents overflow, though this is an artificial bound. The two-step doorway (set flag then clear) is a reasonable abstraction. 1927 distinct states indicates meaningful state exploration. Minor: flag semantics differ slightly from Lamport's original but the safety property holds.

Reasonable key-value store model but the Linearizability invariant is weak. It only checks that lastGet[k] equals store[k] or is 0, which is always true by construction since Get reads directly from store. A true linearizability property would need concurrent operations and ordering. The spec is really just a sequential store with a read cache. Decent TLA+ idiom but the invariant gives false confidence about linearizability.

Ambitious Paxos spec with quorums, ballots, prepare/accept/choose phases. Structure is reasonable. However, the Consistency invariant is too weak - it only checks chosen is in Values, not that once chosen it never changes (which is guaranteed by the guard chosen=-1 in Choose but should be stated). The Accept phase's value selection logic (promisedVals check) is simplified - it should pick the value associated with the highest maxVBal, not just any value in promisedVals. This could allow inconsistency with multiple proposers.

Excellent bounded retransmission spec. Models the full protocol: send, lose data, deliver, lose ack, receive ack, timeout with retry limit. Channel states are well-chosen. DeliveryOrFailure invariant correctly captures the key safety property. State decomposition into clear named actions is exemplary. The Done action properly stutters. Very high training value - teaches realistic protocol modeling with failure handling.

Well-structured snapshot isolation spec with proper transaction lifecycle (begin, write, commit with conflict check, abort). The write-write conflict detection on commit is correct. NoWriteConflict invariant properly captures the safety property. The commit action's conflict check against already-committed transactions is the right approach. Minor: committed values are snapshot+1 rather than explicit write values, but this works for the abstraction. 37899 states indicates thorough exploration. Excellent training example for database concurrency.

Clock synchronization model with averaging approach. The Sync action correctly picks a peer and averages, tracking which nodes have synced. However, ClockBound with range -5..5 is extremely weak for an 'epsilon' bound - with initial clocks in 0..3, integer division averaging should bring them much closer. The invariant is essentially always true and would not catch bugs. Also, each node syncs exactly once with one arbitrary peer, which is a very simplified model of clock sync. Decent structure but weak invariant.

Clean memory allocator spec with proper page pool management. SafeAllocation invariant correctly ensures no page is double-allocated. The free/allocated partition is well-modeled. Allocate and Release actions are clean with proper set operations. Minor: no conservation invariant (free \cup all allocated = Pages) which would strengthen the spec. Good training value for resource management patterns.

Pub-sub broker with subscriptions, publishing, and delivery. Structure is clean. However, DeliveryGuarantee only states delivered <= published, which is trivially maintained by construction (Deliver increments only when delivered < published). A meaningful invariant would be something about eventual delivery or no message loss. The MaxMsgs=1 constant severely limits the model. The Done action does not require all deliveries to complete. Decent pattern but weak invariant.

The best spec in both batches. Proper Dekker's algorithm with full pc-based state machine (idle, set_flag, check, wait, critical, exit). Correctly models the flag-setting, checking, backing-off, and turn-based arbitration of the real algorithm. MutualExclusion invariant is correct and meaningful. Deadlock_Freedom property is a nice bonus. Clean operator decomposition with Other(p) helper. 40 distinct states is appropriate for 2-process Dekker. Excellent training example.

Readers-writers model with integer reader count and boolean writer flag. The transitions allow a reader to be added while writer is true (first disjunct does not check writer=FALSE), which violates the core readers-writers safety property. No invariant is defined beyond TypeOK. The prompt description is completely empty, making this useless as a training pair. Missing the fundamental invariant: ~(writer /\ readers > 0). Would teach incomplete modeling.
